Ge, Xiaohua

coredata.dc:description:
Accurate and rapid fault detection is essential for the safe operation of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. However, conventional fault detection methods dependent on constant thresholds may have false alarms or missing alarms due to the inevitable disturbances resulted from the battery system modeling errors and measurement noises. In this paper, we design a multi-objective nonlinear fault detection observer for lithium-ion batteries, which is robust against disturbances but sensitive to battery multi-fault. We then perform formal stability and
L
∞
/
H
_
performance analysis for the resultant estimation error system. Furthermore, tractable design procedures for the observer gain parameter and an adaptive threshold are derived. Then, via adaptive thresholding, a delicate three-step multi-fault detection scheme is developed to detect the occurrence of battery various faults, including short-circuit faults, current and voltage sensor faults. Finally, the efficacy of the proposed scheme is validated under several experimental case studies involving a variety of faults with their different levels of severity and erroneous SOC initialization.

head.ce:title: Multi-objective nonlinear observer design for multi-fault detection of lithium-ion battery in electric vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Accurate and rapid fault detection is essential for the safe operation of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles. However, conventional fault detection methods dependent on constant thresholds may have false alarms or missing alarms due to the inevitable disturbances resulted from the battery system modeling errors and measurement noises. In this paper, we design a multi-objective nonlinear fault detection observer for lithium-ion batteries, which is robust against disturbances but sensitive to battery multi-fault. We then perform formal stability and
L
∞
/
H
_
performance analysis for the resultant estimation error system. Furthermore, tractable design procedures for the observer gain parameter and an adaptive threshold are derived. Then, via adaptive thresholding, a delicate three-step multi-fault detection scheme is developed to detect the occurrence of battery various faults, including short-circuit faults, current and voltage sensor faults. Finally, the efficacy of the proposed scheme is validated under several experimental case studies involving a variety of faults with their different levels of severity and erroneous SOC initialization.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become the mainstream energy storage for electric vehicles (EVs) due to their advantages of high volumetric energy and power density, long lifespan and low self-discharge rate [1–4]. However, LIBs are beset with safety issues inherent to their energy-dense chemistries and flammable components. Faults in LIBs pose a formidable threat to accurate battery management and safe battery operation, which hinders the rapid development of EVs [5–7]. Thus, substantial efforts have been devoted to fault diagnosis for LIBs with an aim to guarantee their reliability and safety in EV applications [8–10].

The common electrical faults of LIBs can be categorized into two groups: battery faults and sensor faults. Battery faults generally refer to connection fault, overcharge, over-discharge and internal short circuit (ISC) faults. To meet the voltage level and energy level demands, LIBs are connected in series or parallel to form a battery pack. During EV operation, vibrations can potentially lead to the loosening of electrical connections between batteries, causing an increase in the intercell contact resistance. Such battery connection fault can result in an uneven current flow in a battery pack and an increase of heat generation [11]. Overcharge and over-discharge, which are indicated by terminal voltage larger or smaller than the predefined upper or lower cutoff voltage, could lead to undesirable battery side reactions and irreversible damage to battery internal and external structure such as battery swelling and electrolyte leakage [12]. The latter caused by lithium dendrite penetration is the most concerned battery fault due to its imperceptible influence on voltage and temperature response initially induced from soft SC faults and potential for intense heat generation gradually induced from hard SC faults [13–15]. Sensor faults, according to fault types, further include bias faults, gain faults and intermittent faults. Since battery state estimation and fault diagnosis schemes highly depend on the normal operation of sensors, faulty sensors can cause mismanagement of batteries and lead to significant degradation of battery performance [16,17].

Up until now, extensive research has been carried out to tackle a specific type of battery fault, especially ISC faults. For example, Lin et al. [18] proposed a hard SC fault diagnosis method based on the comparison of the voltage difference and the voltage difference variation correlation coefficients with the predefined thresholds. Chang et al. [19] designed a SC fault diagnosis scheme based on the evolution of battery charging voltage ranking within multiple charging sections. The
3 − σ
criterion was then performed on voltage ranking variation to diagnose voltage anomaly. Qiao et al. [20] employed the mean-normalization to amplify voltage characteristics of the SC battery cell and exploited the density-based spatial clustering to detect and locate the faulty cell in a battery pack. Chen et al. [21] devised a two-layer SC fault diagnosis algorithm, which employed the voltage and current for the abrupt electrical signal variation detection in the first layer and calculated the root mean square error between the estimated and measured voltage to determine SC fault severity through comparison with the threshold in the second layer. Xu et al. [22] proposed a threshold-based soft SC fault diagnosis method via a constant voltage charging current analysis, which determined fault occurrence based on the difference between the measured and estimated charging current. For sensor fault, Xia et al. [23] devised a fault-tolerant sensor topology that can isolate and locate voltage sensor faults by measuring multiple cells’ voltage simultaneously in a battery pack. Yu et al. [24] designed a current sensor fault diagnosis method based on a voltage input current output model via an unscented Kalman filter for fault detection and evaluation. Zhang et al. [25] developed a dual time-scale voltage sensor fault detection and isolation method in a series-connected battery pack. The fault detection was implemented via the comparison between the measurement innovation generated from the estimation and measurement in different time scales with the corresponding threshold. A faulty cell is then located through the variance contribution threshold-based method.

Although the effectiveness of the above methods has been demonstrated to certain extent, each of these methods has focused on a specific type of battery fault. The efficacy of such fault diagnosis methods may have a limited application scope to other faults. This thus motivates a feasible fault diagnosis method that can deal with multi-fault in LIBs. For example, Zhang et al. [26] generated the terminal voltage and temperature residuals via an extended Kalman Filter and evaluated the cumulative sum-based residuals by comparing them with the preset thresholds. The fault type was identified according to the fault isolation table. Ma et al. [27] established the principal component analysis model to detect the abnormity in the pack and adopted the parallel component analysis to reconstruct the fault waveform of the battery parameters, which helped to investigate fault causes. Kang et al. [28] proposed an online multi-fault diagnosis based on a cross-style measurement and improved correlation coefficient analysis. The multi-fault detection and isolation were realized through analyzing correlation coefficients of neighboring voltages and currents. It should be pointed out that the multi-fault diagnosis methods mentioned above generally rely on the predefined constant thresholds to detect different types of faults. However, how to select a proper constant threshold is commonly regarded as a challenging issue, which is an inherent tradeoff issue between desired false alarm rate and acceptable missing alarm rate. Additionally, the constant threshold determination often necessitates massive data training and trial-and-error experiments, and further is prone to condition changes due to unpredictable measurement noises and modeling errors. Therefore, it is of great theoretical and practical importance to develop an effective and efficient multi-fault detection method for LIBs when the unknown disturbances (including modeling errors and measurement noises) and battery faults are simultaneously present.
Motivated by the discussions above, in this paper we propose a multi-objective nonlinear observer design method for multi-fault detection of LIBs. The main contributions are summarized as follows:

(1) An
L
∞
/
H
_
multi-objective nonlinear observer is designed based on a general battery model that incorporates both faults and disturbances from modeling errors and measurement noises. The formal stability analysis of the estimation error system under the desired
L
∞
performance (disturbance attenuation) and
H
_
performance (fault sensitivity) is provided.

(2) A three-step multi-fault detection scheme is developed via comparing the residual generated from the designed observer and an adaptive threshold. More specifically, the threshold can adapt itself to avoid the false alarm caused by the erroneous SOC initialization, modeling errors and measurement noises. This is in contrast to the conventional constant threshold determined from extensive trial-and-error simulations and experiments that are exploited to achieve a tradeoff between false alarm and missing alarm.

(3) Several experimental case studies, in terms of different types of fault, including current and voltage sensor faults and ISC faults, under the urban dynamometer driving schedule (UDDS) current profile are provided to demonstrate the safe operation of LIBs in EVs. In particular, different voltage and current sensor faults with their different levels of severity as well as ISC faults with the SC resistances of 10 and 100 Ω at erroneous SOC initialization are examined.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Battery modeling is introduced in Section 2. The multi-objective nonlinear fault detection observer is constructed in Section 3. Section 4 details formal analysis of the stability and
L
∞
/
H
_
performance of the resulting estimation error system, and also presents the main design steps for the proposed observer. Section 5 develops a three-step multi-fault detection scheme. The experimental setup and validation results are given and discussed in Section 6. Finally, Section 7 discusses the conclusions of the study.

ce:section.ce:para: Before ending this section, we summarize the two primary objectives of this study.

ce:list-item.ce:para:

The second objective of this study is to develop a multi-fault detection scheme that can alert the occurrence of multi-fault in the concerned battery system, which will be elaborated in Section 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the fixed observer gain matrix L
ce:section.ce:para: As stated in Section 3, the key to the design of the proposed nonlinear fault detection observer (11) lies in the determination of the fixed gain matrix L . We next summarize the main steps to solve out the gain matrix L .

Some remarkable features of the designed multi-objective nonlinear observer are summarized below. First, the designed observer can handle various types of battery faults, such as ISC faults and sensor faults, via configuring the parameter matrices
B
f
and
D
f
as shown in Table 1. It can be seen that ISC faults and current sensor faults can be characterized by the same
B
f
and
D
f
. This is because that the resultant depletion ISC current has a similar effect with a bias current sensor fault. However, different from the bias current sensor fault which is independent to the variation of terminal voltage, ISC current has a proportional relationship with the terminal voltage. Therefore, to distinguish the above-mentioned two types of faults, we can evaluate the ratio between the estimated faulty current and the terminal voltage for fault identification. Second, as indicated in the design steps above, the observer gain matrix L can be determined offline. Consequently, the proposed observer can be efficiently implemented without necessitating too much effort on the model parameters identification. In contrast, the well-known extended Kalman filter requires iteratively updated equations for determining dynamic observer gain matrices. Third, the proposed observer features a time-varying gain term
h
̇
that is updated online according to the OCV-SOC curve. Such a dynamic gain
L
h
̇
in (11) has the potential to increase the SOC estimation accuracy compared with the persistently constant gain L over the whole range of SOC. On the other hand, the dynamic gain
L
h
̇
also plays a key role in resisting the effects of the disturbances. Whenever the disturbances result in significant measurement variations, the observer gain will be dynamically adjusted to respond to such changes such that the resultant estimates for battery system state may remain accurate.

Based on (65), we then propose the following decision logic for fault detection via comparing the absolute value of residual

Note that some existing fault detection methods in [29–31] determine a constant threshold
r
t h
through massive trial-and-error simulations or experiments. However, the proposed fault detection method can dynamically adjust the threshold by using (65), which significantly reduces the complexity of threshold determination. Additionally, when dealing with the unknown initial error, the conventional constant threshold
r
t h
needs to be optimized based on the tradeoff between the missing alarm rate and the false alarm rate. While the adaptive threshold calculated from (65) can adapt itself to minimize the probability error of diagnosis decisions caused by erroneous initialization.

The proposed method employs the maximum bound of energy to determine the threshold. This conservative approach may lead to the reduced fault detection sensitivity, particularly for mild faults, where the overestimated disturbance could make the residual less apparent for detection, potentially resulting in missed alarms. For severe faults, the influence of the disturbance becomes negligible compared to the significant impact of the fault on the residual. Therefore, setting the threshold based on the worst-case disturbance allows the method to accurately detect severe faults despite the potentially sacrificed sensitivity for detecting mild faults. Overall, the proposed method strikes a balance between fault detection accuracy and sensitivity suitable for real-world applications, where timely tracking of disturbances can be challenging, and different levels of fault severity must be considered.

The ISC fault is emulated by short-circuiting negative and positive terminals of the selected battery via a reheostat as seen in Fig. 3 and the sensor fault is simulated by injecting a fault signal into the raw data measured by the corresponding sensors. Three types of sensor faults are considered, including bias, gain and intermittent faults. Bias and gain faults are represented by injecting signals with fixed deviation and scaling into the raw data, respectively. Intermittent sensor faults are studied in two cases, including zeroing the measurements with 0.5% occurring rate (0 A 0.5%, 0 V 0.5%) and setting the measurements to the maximum current value at 3 A or to the maximum voltage value at 4.2 V with 0.5% occurring rate (3 A 0.5%, 4.2 V 0.5%) after 300 s. All fault tests are performed by repeating the UDDS current profiles until the battery terminal voltage reaches the cut-off value and the initial estimation SOC values of the batteries are set to 95%, which is deviated from their true values of 100%.

To investigate the characteristics of the OCV-SOC curves, we perform the OCV test. A series of pre-designed incremental pulse tests are performed at the selected SOCs. We take the discharge pulse test from 100% SOC to 10% with an interval of 10% SOC. Table 4 lists the coefficients of the OCV-SOC curves.

The set-valued ellipsoid observer is used to identify battery parameters

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of the multi-objective nonlinear fault detection observer
ce:section.ce:para: Scenario 1: Normal operation (fault-free). The residual evaluation for the battery in a normal operation over the entire SOC range is shown in Fig. 5, where the difference between the estimated and measured voltage is indicated in a blue line and the threshold is in a red line. Compared with the conventional constant threshold which has to sacrifice fault detection sensitivity to avoid false alarms caused by initial error, the designed time-varying threshold can adapt itself against the initial SOC error. Furthermore, the residual can be well enclosed by the adaptive threshold, which verifies that the threshold is immune to measurement noises, modeling errors and erroneous SOC initialization.

Scenario 2: Short circuit faults. Fig. 6 demonstrates the validation results of residual evaluation at different SC resistances. The SC resistances are respectively connected to the battery at 340 s and 230 s. The comparisons between the residual and threshold are presented in Fig. 6(a)(b) with the zoomed figures, showing the residual response at the fault triggering state. It can be clearly observed that the residuals are always below the adaptive threshold when fault is not triggered even when the initial SOC estimate is not correct. However, once the fault is triggered, the residual immediately experiences an increase and jumps across the threshold and further triggers a fault alarm, which verifies the efficacy and superiority of the designed adaptive threshold. Additionally, by comparing Fig. 6(a)(b), it can be further concluded that the magnitude of SC resistance affects the fault alarm sensitivity, and the resultant residuals apparently have a proportional relationship with the severity of ISC fault.

Scenario 3: Current sensor faults. Figs. 7–9 show the results for various current sensor faults, including bias fault, gain fault and intermittent fault. It can be seen in Figs. 7–8 that compared with the fault detection for current sensor bias fault, the fault detection for gain fault triggers the alarm less frequently. The reason behind is that the designed threshold is suitable for faults in a certain range. The resultant magnitude of the current sensor gain fault depends on the actual current value, even if the gain is sufficiently large when the true measurement is small, the generated residual still cannot trigger the fault alarm and the residual stay below the threshold. Furthermore, Fig. 9 investigates the residual evaluation results for current sensor intermittent fault. Regarding the intermittent fault with maximizing measurement (3 A 0.5%), the fault detection rate is 100%. While for the intermittent fault with zeroing current (0 A 0.5%), 67% of such intermittent fault can be detected by the proposed method. The zoomed figures in Fig. 9(a)(b) indicated by a green solid line represent the current difference between the actual value and faulty measurement. It can be found that in some case, even there exists an intermittent fault, the residual still cannot trigger the threshold. The reasons are twofold. First, the concerned battery system disturbances including measurement noises and modeling errors are randomly distributed over some unknown ranges instead of keeping constant. Since the threshold is determined based on the assumption of the worst-case disturbance case, the residual sometimes may not trigger the fault when the disturbances are benign. Additionally, the influence of current sensor faults also depends on the internal resistance
R
0
. In this study, a small internal resistance (i.e., 0.0675 Ω ) makes the fault influence on the terminal voltage imperceptibly small compared to voltage sensor faults. Second, the coupling between the battery faults
f
( t )
and disturbances
d
( t )
may inherently cancel out some adverse effects. This then results in the reduction in the generated residuals. In particular, the offset becomes obvious when the two terms affect the battery dynamics in an opposite direction.

Scenario 4: Voltage sensor faults. Regarding the voltage sensor fault, as shown in Figs. 10–12 (a)(b), it indicates that the residual evaluation sensitivity is obviously higher than the current sensor fault, regardless of sensor fault type. In particular for an intermittent fault, the fault detection rate is 100% no matter whether it is the zeroing fault or the maximizing fault. It is because that the main principle of the residual evaluation is making a comparison between the estimated and measured terminal voltage. The influence of voltage sensor fault is directly related to the residual which results in a higher sensitivity.

In summary, according to Figs. 6–8, 10–11, the reaction time for different types of faults is listed in Table 6. “0 s” fault reaction time indicates the instant detection of fault occurrence at the moment when the fault is triggered. From the results, we can deduce the following two points: (1) With the increase in the severity of faults, the designed threshold exhibits high sensitivity and fast reaction to fault occurrence. (2) The designed threshold demonstrates a higher level of responsiveness to voltage sensor faults than current sensor faults and ISC faults. As for fault identification, recalling Table 1, it can be seen that ISC fault and current sensor fault have the same parameter matrices. By comparing Figs. 6–8, when these two types of faults occur, the residual will experience an increase, frequently jump across the threshold and trigger the fault alarm. It is clear that the residual for current sensor faults and ISC faults has a similar trend and thus we cannot identify the current sensor faults and ISC faults only based on the residual response. However, different from current-related faults, voltage sensor faults have a different parameter matrix. According to Figs. 10–11, the residual for voltage sensor faults will have a drastic change when fault is triggered and then return to the normal range. For voltage bias fault, the residual will experience a sudden spike at the occurrence of sensor fault and then revert below the threshold in a fast manner. While for voltage gain fault, due to the variation of voltage fault caused by gain factor, after the spike, the residual of voltage gain fault stabilizes below the threshold in a relatively slow manner. Hence, according to the speed of reverting below the threshold, we can identify the voltage gain fault and bias fault.

In this work, an
L
∞
/
H
_
multi-objective nonlinear observer is developed for multi-fault detection of LiBs in EVs. Firstly, a general first-order RC ECM is constructed to describe electrical characteristic of a battery. Then, a multi-objective nonlinear fault detection observer with
L
∞
and
H
_
performance is designed for state estimation and residual generation. Meanwhile, an adaptive threshold is obtained via
L
∞
analysis. After that, we develop a three-step fault detection scheme, which can effectively detect the occurrence of multiple faults through the comparison between residual and the threshold. Finally, we validate the designed multi-objective nonlinear observer under UDDS with various types of the faults including ISC faults, voltage sensor faults and current sensor faults. Compared with the existing fault diagnosis methods, the proposed method can effectively detect the occurrence of various battery faults, including short-circuit faults, current sensor faults, and voltage sensor faults, and achieve rapid response to the occurrence of various battery faults with high sensitivity while maintaining robustness against disturbances. The experimental results prove that the residual generating from designed nonlinear observer is immune to the disturbances including modeling errors, measurement noises and initial SOC errors but sensitive to fault occurrence in all the studied cases. It is worth noting that both soft ISC faults and capacity aging have a similar effect on battery voltage, current, and SOC. Improving our method to accurately differentiate between these conditions remains a key goal for our future research efforts.

Yiming Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaohua Ge: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Weixiang Shen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
